flags.message: The provisioned snapshot is a bare SCOTUS docket stub: it has zero docket entries and no filing date, termination date, originating court, parties, issue description, or cert disposition fields. The prediction was completed from the sparse snapshot and corpus base-rate context, but the case-specific evidentiary basis is very limited.

Snapshot is maximally sparse: the caption is only the docket number ('No. 90-1397'), with no parties, no docket-number field, no dates, and no docket entries — only a link to one opinion cluster. Unlike sparse dockets with a recognizable caption, the underlying case cannot be identified, so the prediction is purely base-rate driven from corpus stats. If the linked cluster's disposition text were ingested into the corpus/snapshot, this docket would likely be recoverable (probe-recoverability candidate).

The snapshot is materially sparse for a SCOTUS petition-disposition prediction: it has no docket entries, docket number, lower-court metadata, cert-stage dates, question presented, parties, or petition text. It does include a linked opinion cluster, which may indicate an opinion-based historical record rather than a clean petition-order record. The prediction is therefore mostly base-rate calibrated with low confidence.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare bulk-import docket: no docket entries, no dates, no docket number, and no petition documents. The only case-specific signals are the caption 'Bryan v. United States' and one opinion cluster — and the caption matches at least two distinct SCOTUS merits cases (338 U.S. 552 (1950) and 524 U.S. 184 (1998)), which the snapshot cannot disambiguate. The prediction rests on the observation that both candidates resolve identically (certiorari granted, judgment affirmed against the petitioner) plus corpus base rates.

The snapshot is a bare SCOTUS record: no docket number, docket entries, lower-court metadata, filing dates, cert dates, parties, attorneys, or petition text. It includes a linked cluster, which may indicate a published-opinion record rather than a clean modern discretionary-cert petition. The prediction is therefore mostly base-rate calibrated.

The snapshot is a bare bulk-scrape docket stub: no docket entries, no docket number, no dates, null cert-granted/cert-denied fields, with one linked opinion cluster. This is the profile of a pre-certiorari-era (mandatory jurisdiction) SCOTUS case, so a petition-disposition event does not map cleanly onto it — it looks like the population the predict scope's pre-1925 exclusion targets, but the docket's missing dates likely prevented the exclusion predicate from classifying it. Consider a recoverability probe or latching the scope exclusion for this docket.

The snapshot is a bare SCOTUS stub: no docket number, docket entries, lower-court metadata, filing dates, cert dates, parties, or petition text. It includes a linked cluster, which may indicate a published-opinion record rather than a clean modern discretionary-cert petition. The prediction is therefore mostly base-rate calibrated.

This looks like a #363-class case (historical SCOTUS docket, published opinion linked, outcome only in opinion text) that escaped the is_published_opinion_unresolvable exclusion: the docket snapshot carries a cluster link, but the corpus row's citations/citation_count/opinion_text are all empty, so the predicate cannot see the opinion. The empty docket number also defeats the #309 and #333 predicates. A cluster-detail backfill (or extending the #363 signal to the docket's cluster link) would take this class out of predict scope at the source.

Snapshot is metadata-only: empty docket number, no docket entries, no parties/attorneys, every activity date null. The prediction rests on the linked-opinion-cluster signal plus corpus base rates (resolved SCOTUS events are 78% 'other'), not on any petition-stage facts.
Snapshot contains no docket entries, dates, or lower court info. It is a stub for 'Minor v. Tillotson'. Cannot make an informed prediction; falling back to base rate.

The snapshot is a sparse SCOTUS docket with no docket number, entries, or cert dates, but it has a linked opinion cluster. Repository scope notes describe this historical/opinion-only shape as a mismatch for the modern discretionary-cert event model, so the prediction was completed conservatively as `other`.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare bulk-import docket: no docket entries, no dates, no docket number, and no petition documents. The only case-specific signal is the caption and one opinion cluster. The prediction therefore rests on recognizing the caption as the historical writ-of-error case Minor v. Tillotson (Louisiana land-title litigation, reported at 32 U.S. 99 (1833) and again in the 1840s), plus corpus base rates; a predictor without background knowledge of this case could only emit the raw base rate.

This case predates the certiorari system, so a petition-disposition event has no natural granted/denied reading; the disposition enum lacks affirmed/reversed labels, and corpus stats show ~78% of resolved SCOTUS events reconcile to 'other'. Choosing between 'other' and 'denied' is mostly a guess about the reconciler's label mapping rather than legal uncertainty. A documented mapping for historical merits dispositions would make these events crisper.

The case snapshot contains no docket entries, date_filed is null, and there are no details about the petition beyond the case name 'Charles W. Castleman, a in Bankruptcy'. The prediction relies entirely on the SCOTUS baseline denial rate.

The snapshot is valid but very sparse for a SCOTUS petition-disposition prediction: it has no docket number, docket entries, filing/termination dates, lower-court origin, petition text, issue statement, or cert-grant/denial date. The prediction is therefore mostly base-rate driven.

Bare legacy-import docket: no entries, no dates, no docket number, and an OCR-mangled caption ('Charles W. Castleman, a in Bankruptcy'). Only usable facts are the caption and one linked opinion cluster. Given a corpus prior (scotus/1006) that is a misattributed New York Court of Appeals record, this record may not be a SCOTUS matter at all. A recoverability probe (fedcourts probe-recoverability) on this docket would tell whether the cluster carries a disposition.

Caption style ('a Bankrupt' / 'in Bankruptcy') indicates an 1867-Act-era or early-1898-Act-era bankruptcy matter, i.e. almost certainly pre-1925 mandatory jurisdiction, where no discretionary cert petition existed to grant or deny. The empty docket number likely kept the pre-1925 scope exclusion from classifying it, so it stayed in the predict matrix.

The provisioned snapshot is sparse for a petition-disposition prediction: it has no docket number, docket entries, filing or termination dates, cert-grant or cert-denial dates, originating court, issue statement, or party details beyond the case name. The prediction therefore relies mainly on the case name, one cluster reference, and aggregate corpus base rates.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare bulk-import docket: no docket entries, no dates, no docket number, and no petition documents. The only case-specific signal is the caption and one opinion cluster. The prediction therefore rests on recognizing the caption as the historical case Bartemeyer v. Iowa, 85 U.S. 129 (1874), plus corpus base rates; a predictor without background knowledge of this case could only emit the raw base rate.

This is a 19th-century writ-of-error merits case; the disposition enum (granted/denied/...) has no affirmed/reversed labels, and corpus stats show ~78% of resolved SCOTUS events reconcile to 'other'. Choosing between 'other' and 'denied' is mostly a guess about the reconciler's label mapping rather than legal uncertainty. A documented mapping for historical merits dispositions would make these events crisper.

The provisioned snapshot for this SCOTUS petition-disposition event is very sparse: it has no docket entries, filing or disposition dates, cert-grant or cert-denial dates, lower-court metadata, petition-stage text, or visible opinion-cluster contents. I completed the prediction from the available snapshot and local corpus priors, but confidence is materially limited.

Snapshot is nearly empty: no docket entries, no filing/termination dates, no lower-court or party detail. The only predictive signals are the case caption and the pre-1971 miscellaneous docket number ('979, Misc'). The prediction is therefore essentially a calibrated base-rate call; if richer snapshots are available for these historical bulk-import dockets (even just a filing Term), they would materially improve prediction quality.
Snapshot lacks docket entries, docket number, filing dates, originating court, lower-court metadata, party detail, and issue/conflict information; prediction is necessarily base-rate driven.

This appears to be an antebellum mandatory-jurisdiction cause (Montgomery v. Hernandez, 12 Wheat. 129 (1827), a section-25 writ of error), not a cert petition, so the petition-disposition frame does not really apply. It seems to have stayed in predict scope because the snapshot and corpus row carry no dates or docket number for the era-based exclusion predicates to latch onto.

The snapshot is nearly bare: no docket entries, dates, docket number, or parties; the only substantive signals are the case name and one linked opinion cluster. The disposition looks recoverable from that cluster (a probe-recoverability candidate), which would let this event be resolved or excluded instead of predicted from a one-line record.

exclusions.reason: SCOTUS application / original-jurisdiction docket — not discretionary cert (#362)
published opinion but no machine-readable cert disposition (#363)

unclassified.reason: docket Term not parseable (a format the predicate skips)

gaps.label: A documented disposition-label mapping for historical merits cases (affirmed/reversed -> other?) to remove label-choice guesswork
The provisioned snapshot exposed only a cluster URL, not the cluster metadata or petition-stage order text.
a derived era/Term field on the case row so historical cases can be base-rated against their own period
fedcourts query rows mostly lack case_name/date_filed/topic, which makes it hard to judge whether a prior is actually similar
no way to filter stats/query to cert-stage petition outcomes specifically; the resolved-label mix ('other'-dominated) is hard to interpret for a petition event
semantic/topic retrieval over historical SCOTUS priors would beat the structured filters here (no topic, judges, or citations on this stub docket to filter by)
surfacing the linked opinion cluster's non-outcome metadata (decision date, reporter citation) in the snapshot when the docket itself is bare
A --docket-number-class or motion-type filter (e.g. SCOTUS M-docket vs. cert docket) for query/stats; the M-docket population has a very different base rate than the cert docket
A --term or date cut was unusable because the snapshot has no filing date to anchor it

A Term/era estimate derived at ingest for old Miscellaneous Docket numbers (even a decade bucket) would let the predictor use era-specific base rates instead of inferring the era from the docket-number style.

helpful.label: Schema files made the artifact contract clear.
fedcourts paths resolved the event path.
fedcourts paths: confirmed resolved output locations
fedcourts paths confirmed the event and outcome locations.
fedcourts paths for resolving the output layout

gaps: A read-only recoverability indicator for bare SCOTUS petition stubs would help separate genuinely absent upstream facts from ingestion gaps without fetching new case facts during prediction.
A petition-specific SCOTUS cert-grant statpack by term and originating court would be more useful than the very small resolved slice available here.
helpful: Path resolution identified the event and provisioned snapshot locations.
Base-rate stats gave a calibration anchor when the snapshot had no merits facts.
recent.notes: The prediction relied heavily on base-rate calibration because the snapshot contained no docket entries or issue-level facts.

gaps: Snapshot inclusion of the linked opinion cluster's disposition text (affirmed/reversed/cert-denied) would let sparse cluster-backed dockets be predicted from evidence rather than pure base rates
A stats cut conditioned on 'docket has an opinion cluster' vs not would sharpen the prior for this docket shape
helpful: fedcourts stats --group-by term_year showed modern-Term cluster-backed dockets resolve to 'other' 25/26, the decisive calibration input
fedcourts stats --court scotus gave the overall disposition base rates
fedcourts query surfaced structurally similar sparse priors confirming the docket shape
recent.notes: Snapshot had no caption/parties/dates, so corpus base rates carried the entire prediction; --term filtering could not match this docket because its docket_number field is empty (the number lives only in case_name).

gaps: Snapshot lacks docket entries, docket number, cert-stage dates, lower-court metadata, question presented, and petition text.
Semantic search over petition text or cluster summaries would help when structured filters are unavailable.
helpful: Schema files made the artifact contract clear.
fedcourts paths confirmed the event location.
fedcourts stats provided SCOTUS base-rate calibration.
fedcourts query exposed the limits of structured priors for sparse snapshots.
recent.notes: The base-rate tool was useful, but this case had too little structured metadata for targeted priors beyond court-level SCOTUS context.

gaps: A way to pull the target case's own opinion-cluster text (or U.S. Reports citation) from the corpus — the snapshot's caption matches at least two distinct SCOTUS merits cases and nothing on-runner disambiguates them
A documented disposition-label mapping for historical merits cases (affirmed/reversed -> other?) so the label choice is a rule, not an inference from base rates
helpful: fedcourts stats --court scotus --resolved-only: the disposition split (78% 'other', 1.4% 'granted') directly set both the label and the probability
fedcourts query --disposition granted: showed the rare 'granted' labels attach to writ-granting opinions, not ordinary certiorari merits cases
fedcourts paths: confirmed resolved output locations
recent.notes: Base-rate stats again did the decisive work for a bare opinion-import stub; the ledger's prior claude-baseline runs on same-shaped dockets were also useful as calibration precedent.

gaps: Snapshot lacks question presented, docket entries, cert-stage history, lower-court metadata, and petition text.
A semantic corpus prior over cluster or opinion text would help distinguish sparse historical records from modern cert petitions.
helpful: Schema files made the artifact contract clear.
fedcourts paths confirmed the event and outcome locations.
fedcourts stats provided useful SCOTUS base-rate calibration.
recent.notes: The base-rate tooling was useful. The structured corpus query was only lightly useful because the snapshot has no filterable legal features beyond court and sparse docket metadata.

gaps: A way to query the corpus for priors filtered to the same docket profile (bare stub, opinion-cluster-bearing, no dates) to sharpen the disposition mapping for historical cases
query lacks a filter to look up a single case row by court/docket, so there is no sanctioned way to see the corpus's own metadata (topic, predict_excluded) for the case being predicted
helpful: fedcourts stats resolved base rates were the entire calibration signal for this bare snapshot
fedcourts query priors confirmed comparable opinion-cluster dockets resolve as other/dismissed
recent.notes: Snapshot had no petition-stage facts at all, so the prediction is pure base-rate calibration; stats and query carried the run.

gaps: Snapshot lacks question presented, docket entries, cert-stage history, and lower-court metadata.
Corpus query has only structured filters; semantic similarity over petition text would help when snapshots are sparse.
helpful: Schema files made the artifact contract clear.
fedcourts paths confirmed the event and outcome locations.
fedcourts stats provided useful SCOTUS base-rate calibration.
recent.notes: The base-rate tool was useful. The structured query was not materially helpful for this case because the snapshot had no filterable legal features beyond court.

gaps: A stats/query cut for 'has linked opinion cluster' — the single most predictive feature of this snapshot has no corresponding corpus filter, so the conditional base rate had to be reasoned qualitatively

Cluster-detail enrichment in the snapshot or corpus row: the docket links a cluster but no citation/disposition/opinion fields are populated, which is also why the #363 scope exclusion missed this case
The --term filter was unusable: the docket number is empty so no Term year exists to anchor it
helpful: fedcourts stats --court scotus --resolved-only for the disposition base rate (78% 'other'), which drove the disposition call
fedcourts query --court scotus to confirm the resolved reference class is digitized historical dockets like this one
fedcourts paths to resolve output locations
recent.notes: Reading the scope predicates in src/fedcourtsai/corpus.py was the most informative step — it identified this case as an escapee of the #363 exclusion, which reframed the task from 'predict a cert petition' to 'predict the disposition label of an already-decided historical case'.
